fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the auto-lock timing feature sorted out for the Android app. Users have been complaining that the app either locks too aggressively or never seems to lock at all depending on which option they pick in settings. The lock screen choices aren't mapping to the right wait times on the backend, which is causing confusing behavior. Can we make sure all the duration options are properly wired up?

Also while we're in there — remember how we handled the 'never lock' edge case in that settings screen we did a while back? We should follow the same pattern here. The 'Never' option needs special treatment since it's not really a time value.

The output should just expose each named choice paired with its numeric value so the rest of the system can consume it cleanly. The full list of options should be supported: the short ones like one minute and five minutes, the medium ones, and the longer overnight-style ones. Make sure the never-lock option doesn't accidentally get a real millisecond value assigned to it — that's been a source of bugs before.

This is blocking the QA team from signing off on the lock screen settings flow, so please prioritize. Should be straightforward if we follow the existing conventions.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for this mapping, the complete set needs to be exactly OneMinute, FiveMinutes, FifteenMinutes, ThirtyMinutes, OneHour, TwelveHours, TwentyFourHours, and Never. All eight should be supported and all eight should produce output entries so nothing gets skipped depending on which option the user picked.

On the values, we should wire them exactly as follows, all in milliseconds: OneMinute=60000, FiveMinutes=300000, FifteenMinutes=900000, ThirtyMinutes=1800000, OneHour=3600000, TwelveHours=43200000, TwentyFourHours=86400000. And just to be extra clear on the special case, Never must be represented as exactly zero (0 milliseconds), not a sentinel like -1 or Long.MAX_VALUE. Any non-zero value for Never would be treated as a real timeout by the consuming system, which is the bug we want to avoid.

This should line up with the auto_lock_durations feature contract in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature8_auto_lock_durations.json, specifically the rule that Never must map to 0 milliseconds rather than any non-zero or sentinel value.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format
how should the results be emitted
line-oriented output structure for durations
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each duration choice must be emitted as a separate line in the format 'Name=value\n', preserving the exact casing of the duration name as supplied in the input list. The order of output lines must match the order of the input list.

trigger_keywords: what is the input to this feature
how are durations passed in
does the feature take a fixed list or a dynamic one
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The feature accepts a list of duration name strings as input (e.g. from a JSON array field 'durations'). The system looks up the millisecond value for each named entry and emits it. The caller is responsible for supplying the desired set; the engine maps names to values.

trigger_keywords: what is the never-lock special pattern from the previous settings screen
reference to the compatibility logic from last time
what existing convention should be followed for Never
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The vague reference to 'the way we handled Never last time' points to the auto_lock_durations feature contract defined in feature8_auto_lock_durations.json under rcb_tests/public_test_cases. The convention is: Never maps to 0L (zero as a Long), distinguishing it from real timeouts while still being a valid numeric type consistent with all other entries in the map.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Use the same line-oriented key=value output format used across all other features in the project (e.g. feature1 through feature7), where each named duration is a separate 'Name=millis' line ending with a newline character
TwelveHours (43200000 ms) and TwentyFourHours (86400000 ms) — the two durations that span half a day or a full day respectively

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at how similar features handle their output format? The pattern should be consistent with the rest of the codebase.

feature1_credential_summary.json.description: Convert a stored credential into a list-row summary with a display title, subtitle, and stable identifier.

feature2_credential_detail.json.description: Convert a stored credential into a detail record preserving origin, username, password, and whether a visible username exists.

cases.expected_output:
id=id0
title=mozilla.org
hostname=https://www.mozilla.org
username=dogs@dogs.com
password=woof
has_username=true

feature3_1_domain_from_web_domain.json.description: Resolve a web domain string to its registrable top domain while preserving the full supplied domain and empty status.

feature3_2_domain_from_package_name.json.description: Resolve an application package identifier by reversing its labels into a domain and then deriving the registrable top domain.

feature3_3_domain_from_origin.json.description: Resolve a URL origin by extracting its host and deriving the registrable top domain, falling back to an empty result when no valid host is available.

feature3_4_domain_match.json.description: Compare two resolved domains as matching when their registrable top domains are equal, regardless of subdomain differences.

feature3_5_filter_credentials.json.description: Filter saved credentials to those whose stored host resolves to the same registrable domain as the requesting web domain or package-derived domain.

feature4_autofill_fields.json.description: Parse a view tree to identify username and password fill targets from fill hints, visible labels, HTML inputs, and nearby package or web origin clues.

feature5_history_stack.json.description: Maintain a replayable history stack that emits pushed values to subscribers and replays only the current top value to new subscribers; stack mutation operations do not emit by themselves.

feature6_route_reducer.json.description: Reduce navigation and data-state events into the current screen route, ignoring data error states while emitting explicit external route events.

feature7_lock_foreground_auth.json.description: Track whether biometric authentication may be launched when the app comes to foreground based on lock, background, and unlocking events; pass authentication events through.

feature8_auto_lock_durations.json.description: Expose the configured auto-lock choices as millisecond durations, with the never-lock choice represented by zero.

cases.expected_output:
OneMinute=60000
FiveMinutes=300000
FifteenMinutes=900000
ThirtyMinutes=1800000
OneHour=3600000
TwelveHours=43200000
TwentyFourHours=86400000
Never=0

feature9_read_text_file.json.description: Read the complete contents of a text file and normalize missing-file failures to a language-neutral error category.
